text: Which of the following encryption algorithms has the largest number of possible keys?

options.text: DES (the Data Encryption Standard algorithm)
The random simple substitution cipher on an alphabet of 26 characters
A transposition cipher on blocks of size 10
The Vigenére cipher with a key of length 5 and an alphabet of 26 characters

text: Following Kerckhoff’s principle, we usually assume that an attacker of an encryption scheme has access to:

options.text: unbounded computational power
the encryption and decryption keys
the description of the encryption and decryption algorithms
all of the above.

text: Double encryption with DES (double DES) with two independent keys:

options.text: has twice as many possible key values as ordinary DES
uses half as much computation as ordinary DES
runs twice as fast as ordinary DES
is vulnerable to a meet-in-the-middle attack

text: The AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) algorithm:

options.text: has a 128 bit block size
has a 192 bit block size
has a 256 bit block size
allows any of the above block sizes

options.text: CBC mode
CTR mode

text: A message authentication code (MAC) provides the security service:

options.text: availability

text: Which of the following is not a binary synchronous stream cipher?

options.text: the one-time pad
RC4

text: For numbers of a similar size, and for currently known algorithms:

options.text: factorising numbers is harder than finding prime numbers
finding prime numbers is harder than factorising numbers
finding prime numbers and factorising numbers are about the same difficulty
the best factorisation and prime generation methods use the same algorithm

text: The Fermat test and the Miller-Rabin test are two tests for deciding whether or not a number is prime. Which of the following statements is true?

options.text: The Miller-Rabin test is more reliable than the Fermat test
The Fermat test is more reliable than the Miller-Rabin test
The Fermat test and the Miller-Rabin test always give the same result
The Fermat test and the Miller-Rabin test always give opposite results

text: The keys for the RSA encryption algorithm include a public exponent e, a private exponent d, and a public modulus n. It is common to choose:

options.text: e = n -1
d = n -1

text: The Diffie-Hellman protocol can be broken by an attacker who is able to:

options.text: solve the discrete logarithm problem
generate large prime numbers

options.text: 2199 elements have been hashed
220 elements have been hashed

text: A construction for a message authentication code from any hash function, often used in TLS, is known as:

options.text: CMAC
HMAC

text: When public key cryptography is used to provide digital signatures:

options.text: the public key of the signer is required in order to verify the signature
the public key of the verifier is required in order to verify the signature
the private key of the signer is required in order to verify the signature
the private key of the verifier is required in order to verify the signature

text: In order to produce a digital certificate, a certification authority computes:

options.text: an encryption of the subject’s private key and identity
an encryption of the subject’s public key and identity
a signature on the subject’s private key and identity
a signature on the subject’s public key and identity

text: Forward secrecy is the property that:

options.text: if a user’s long term key becomes known to an attacker, session keys established earlier are not compromised
if a user’s long term key becomes known to an attacker, session keys established later are not compromised
if a user’s session key becomes known to an attacker, that user’s long term key is not compromised
if a user’s session key becomes known to an attacker, that user’s long term key is also compromised

text: Which of these statements about compression in the TLS record protocol is true?

options.text: Compression of payload is essential to provide extra security
Compression of payload can result in known attacks
Compression is only applied to headers, not to payload
Compression is mandatory in all versions of TLS

text: How is the ciphersuite used in a run of the TLS protocol decided?

options.text: It is chosen by the server
It is chosen by the client
It is negotiated between client and server
It is defined by the latest version of TLS

TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
TLS_DH_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256

text: One common way of designing modern block ciphers is the substitution-permutation network. Each round of such a cipher has three steps: a substitution, a permutation, and a step involving the round key. Explain briefly the operation of each of these steps on the current block state.

parts.text: One common way of designing modern block ciphers is the substitution-permutation network. Each round of such a cipher has three steps: a substitution, a permutation, and a step involving the round key. Explain briefly the operation of each of these steps on the current block state.
parts.solution: The description should describe how substitution replaces sub-blocks in the state; permutation moves around each bit of the block state; sub-key is XOR added to current block state.

text: One mode of operation for block ciphers is counter mode (CTR). The general equation for computing each output block is: Ct = Ot ⊕Pt where Ot = E(Tt, K) and Tt = N∥t is the concatenation of a nonce N and block number t. Suppose that for a certain faulty implementation the nonce N is always fixed for every message. Explain how an attacker can successfully attack this implementation using a known plaintext attack.

parts.text: One mode of operation for block ciphers is counter mode (CTR). The general equation for computing each output block is: Ct = Ot ⊕Pt where Ot = E(Tt, K) and Tt = N∥t is the concatenation of a nonce N and block number t. Suppose that for a certain faulty implementation the nonce N is always fixed for every message. Explain how an attacker can successfully attack this implementation using a known plaintext attack.
parts.solution: Since the nonce N is fixed it means that the values Ot are the same for each message. In a known plaintext attack the attacker knows Pt and Ct for each t and can therefore obtain the Ot values by simply computing Ot = Pt ⊕Ct. Then for another message P ' 1, P ' 2, . . . the attacker can obtain P ' t = Ot ⊕C' t.

text: In the RSA encryption algorithm the public key is a modulus n and public exponent e where n = pq is the product of two large prime numbers. The private key is the exponent d.

parts.text: Show how an attacker who can factorise the modulus n can break the encryption algorithm.
parts.solution: An attacker who can factorise n obtains p and q and can therefore compute φ(n) = (p - 1)(q -1) and hence obtain the private key d = e-1 mod φ(n).

parts.text: What are reasonable choices today for the size of n which are both secure and reasonably efficient? How will this change if full-scale quantum computers become available?
parts.solution: Reasonable choices are between 1024 and 2048 bits. It should be noted that too large mod- ulus affects eﬃciency badly, while lower than 1024 risks factorisation. Realistic quantum computers will make factorisation polynomial time and there is no practical size which can work any longer.

text: The Elgamal signature on a message m is a triple (m, r, s) where r = gk mod p s = k-1(m -xr) mod (p -1) for a random k such that gcd(k, p -1) = 1. Here y = gx mod p is the public key corresponding to the private key x. The corresponding verification equation is gm ?= rsyr mod p.

parts.text: Show that the verification equation works for a valid signature.
parts.solution: If (r, s) is a valid signature then m = ks + xr mod (p -1) and so gm = g(ks+xr) = gksgxr = rsyr.

parts.text: Show that if a signer uses the same random k value to sign two different messages, then the signer’s private key can be found.
parts.solution: The adversary will see two signatures (m1, r, s1) and (m2, r, s2) where r = gk mod p and s1 = k-1(m1 -xr) mod (p -1), s2 = k-1(m2 -xr) mod (p -1). Therefore s1 -s2 = k-1(m1 -m2) or k = m1-m2 s1-s2 mod (p -1). Now that k is found the adversary can compute x = r-1(m1 -ks1) mod (p -1).

text: Fermat’s theorem states that ap-1 mod p = 1 when p is a prime and gcd(a, n) = 1.

parts.text: Illustrate the theorem using one example with respect to the prime p = 23.

Fermat’s theorem can be used as the basis of a test to distinguish prime numbers from composite numbers. Explain the basic operation of such an algorithm and illustrate its use for the non-prime n = 15.
parts.solution: The Fermat test for a number n is to check whether an-1 mod n = 1 for a random a. If not then n is composite. If so then repeat with further a. 24 mod 15 ≡1. Therefore 212 mod 15 ≡1 and so 214 mod 15 ≡4 ̸= 1. So the test recognises that 15 is composite.

text: Written question 6.

parts.text: Explain the main similarities and differences between a message authentication code (MAC) and a hash function.
parts.solution: Both a MAC and a hash function take a message of (practically) any size and output a tag or digest. However, a MAC includes also a key in the calculation so that computation of the correct tag requires knowledge of the key.

parts.text: Why does using the hash of a message instead of a MAC tag fail to provide the properties of a secure MAC?
parts.solution: If a hash tag were used instead of a MAC it can be computed by anybody. An attacker could change a transmitted value in any desired way and compute the correct hash itself.

text: The following message exchange shows a simplified version of the messages exchanged between the client (C) and the authentication server (AS) in the Kerberos protocol. C AS - IDC, IDtgs, N1 {KC,tgs, IDtgs, N1}KC, tickettgs "! # "! # where tickettgs = {KC,tgs, IDC, T1}Ktgs for some validity period T1.

parts.text: What is the purpose of the nonce N1 in this message exchange? How is it used?
parts.solution: Nonce N1 is used to prevent replay attacks. It is generated by C and returned in the message from AS cryptographically bound to the key KC,tgs. C will check that the value returned is the same as it sent in the first message. This allows C to verify that this key is not replayed from a previous run of the protocol.

parts.text: Why does the identity IDtgs need to be included in the response message? What could happen if it were omitted?
parts.solution: IDtgs is checked by C to ensure that the key returned from AS is valid for the correct (ticket granting) server. If it were omitted then an attacker could change the identity in the first message before it got to AS and then C would accept the key for the wrong server.

text: Consider the following ciphersuite specification for TLS: TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256 Explain the meaning of each of the elements in the specification, including an indication of which parts are relevant for the Handshake Protocol and which parts are relevant for the Record Protocol.

parts.text: Consider the following ciphersuite specification for TLS: TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256 Explain the meaning of each of the elements in the specification, including an indication of which parts are relevant for the Handshake Protocol and which parts are relevant for the Record Protocol.
parts.solution: The handshake protocol will use RSA to transport the pre-master key from the client to the server. The record protocol will use AES with 128-bit key and CBC mode of operation and also SHA-256 for HMAC construction.
